attributes.stem: A 77-year-old woman undergoes a cricopharyngeal myotomy and excision of a pharyngeal pouch. The patient has a fixed flexion deformity of her cervical spine, making surgery difficult. Four hours after the operation her neck appears to be swollen and she has developed worsening stridor. On examination, her pulse rate is 102/min, blood pressure 105/85mmHg, temperature 37.1°C and SpO2 94% on 10L Hudson mask. She remains in the recovery unit.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to oxygen therapy, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Explore the wound at the bedside
Rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation
attributes.explanation:
The presence of stridor in this patient indicates laryngeal obstruction. Airway obstruction can develop in patients after neck surgeries and may be caused by extrinsic factors (eg, neck haematoma) or intrinsic factors (eg, laryngeal oedema). This patient's neck swelling and worsening stridor are suggestive of impending airway obstruction and should be dealt with immediately. Delay in immediate management can lead to respiratory failure. The most important first step is to prevent any further progress of airway obstruction and maintenance of patent airway. Any delay may cause further narrowing of the upper airway and make subsequently attempted intubation impossible. The most appropriate management in this patient should be the removal of sutures (to relieve the pressure caused by haematoma) and the emergency transfer of the patient to the theatre for awake fibre-optic intubation. Sutures can be removed at the bedside and if necessary, the wound probed to facilitate decompression.

Ultrasound neck is not advised since it will cause unnecessary delay and will not provide useful information.

A nasopharyngeal airway will not relieve the obstruction and would be inappropriate in this setting.

Exploring the wound at the bedside may be an appropriate option of management if this patient had a severely acute airway obstruction due to rapidly expanding haematoma with no possibility of endotracheal intubation due to a severely narrowed airway. This patient is still conscious with a partial laryngeal obstruction (evidenced by her stridor). Preference should be given to transferring the patient to theatre for exploration under general anaesthetic following an awake fibre-optic intubation.

A rapid sequence induction and endotracheal intubation in this patient with fixed flexion deformity of the neck and airway obstruction from haematoma carries a very high risk of a "can't intubate, can't ventilate" scenario. As she is awake and co-operative with adequate oxygenation priority should be given to transferring the patient to theatre for an urgent awake fibre-optic intubation.
learningPoints: Post-operative neck haematoma with signs of airway obstruction (eg. stridor) should be identified promptly and managed by draining the haematoma to relieve the pressure.
references: Batuwitage B, Charters P. Postoperative management of the difficult airway. BJA Education. 2017;17(7):235-241. Available at: https://academic.oup.com/bjaed/article/17/7/235/2999282
